name: Ma'at (Goddess)
shortDescription: Ma'at is the ancient Egyptian concept of truth, balance, order, law, morality, and justice. She is personified as a goddess who embodies these principles.
description: Ma'at is a central concept in ancient Egyptian philosophy and religion, representing the fundamental order of the cosmos and the harmonious relationship between all things. As a goddess, Ma'at embodies truth, justice, balance, and cosmic order. She is often depicted with an ostrich feather, which was used to weigh against the heart of the deceased in the Hall of Judgement in the afterlife. If the heart was lighter than the feather, the deceased was deemed worthy of entering the afterlife; if heavier, it was consumed by Ammit, the devourer. This judgement symbolized the importance of living a life in accordance with Ma'at. The pharaoh was responsible for upholding Ma'at on Earth, ensuring the well-being of the kingdom and its people. Maintaining Ma'at meant preventing chaos (Isfet) from disrupting the natural order. Ma'at governed not only the physical world but also social interactions, ethical behavior, and religious practices. The concept is analogous to similar ideas in other cultures, such as Dharma in Hinduism, Asha in Zoroastrianism, and Dike in ancient Greek thought, although Ma'at encompasses a broader range of interconnected principles. Rituals and offerings were performed to honor Ma'at and reinforce her presence in the world, demonstrating the Egyptians' commitment to maintaining cosmic equilibrium.

sources.title: Religion and Magic in Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Rosalie David
